nativeRange.habitat: The Pygmy Cherub Angelfish is a reef-associated species, generally reported from rubble zones and rocky outcrops at depths of about 5 to 30 m, occasionally down to around 80 m, where it tends to stay close to refuge holes and crevices.

diet.notes: In the wild, the species is documented grazing on various filamentous and turf algae; in captivity it is generally fed as an omnivore. Offer Spirulina, dried marine algae sheets, quality angelfish preparations, and meaty items such as mysis or finely chopped frozen shrimp. Two to three small feedings per day are commonly recommended, alongside an established tank with live rock for supplemental grazing.

avoidWith: very passive fish that may be bullied

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke; 2340x1584. Tack-sharp full-body shot of a vibrant adult showing deep sapphire-blue flanks, warm orange face and purple eye ring with blue fin margins - textbook diagnostic view.
Justin Philbois; 2048x1536. In-situ Caribbean reef shot, whole fish in profile against red sponge, blue body and orange face clearly visible; slightly shadowed but in focus.
Jay Brasher; 1800x1351. In-situ shot of a vivid oval-bodied individual with bright blue flanks and orange face; whole fish framed, good colour, mild underwater haze.

summary: A diminutive Caribbean dwarf angelfish with deep sapphire-blue flanks and a warm orange face. Generally considered semi-aggressive and most at home in an aged reef tank with abundant live rock for grazing and refuge.
careNotes: The Pygmy Cherub Angelfish is the only Centropyge species native to the Western Atlantic and is commonly kept in nano and small reef aquariums. Despite its small adult size of around 5–8 cm, it tends to be highly territorial toward other dwarf angelfish and especially toward conspecific males; keep only one individual per tank unless introducing an established mated pair simultaneously. It is generally considered reef-safe with caution: individuals have been known to nip at large-polyped stony coral polyps, soft coral, and clam mantles, though many specimens prove well-behaved. Providing numerous caves and hiding places within a well-established live rock aquascape helps reduce stress and territorial behavior. Introduce the Pygmy Cherub Angelfish as one of the last fish added to a peaceful community, and avoid copper-based medications.
breedingNotes: Has been spawned and reared in captivity, including by specialist breeders, but is not commonly available as captive-bred at the hobbyist level. Pair-spawning species; wild populations occur in monogamous home-ranging pairs. Sex differences are subtle — males tend to be slightly larger and may show more intense facial coloration. Eggs are pelagic, and larval rearing requires very small first foods.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range, adult size (FishBase 8.0 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 3 in/7.6 cm — strong agreement; range set to 5–8 cm), temperature (FishBase 23.5–28.1°C vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F/22.2–25.6°C — overlapping; averaged to 23–27°C), and reproduction (FishBase: monogamous home-ranging pairs).

additional.name: Aquifarm – Atlantic Pygmy Angelfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Used to cross-check tank-size recommendations (30 gal absolute minimum, 55 gal aged tank preferred), lifespan (5–7 years), and broader temperature tolerance (72–82°F). Tank decision biased upward to 200 L per playbook nice-values rule, reflecting need for grazing surface and refuge.

fish.breedingNotes: The Pygmy Cherub Angelfish is a pelagic spawner, meaning eggs are released into the water column rather than deposited on a surface. Captive larval rearing has been achieved by specialist breeders but remains beyond most hobbyist setups.
